sentences.text: Well, I saw most of the atrocities.
War-related atrocities that were committed by the SS.
And the German army, the Wehrmacht, they call it.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
The soldiers?
Oh, yes, I met and talked with Germans.
They-- many of them just seemed to turn a deaf ear to it.
They didn't want to hear about it, the civilians that I met over there.
And many of them were aware of it.
And many of them were not aware of what was going on in those concentration camps.
Yes.
Yes.
They had brains pickled in formaldehyde, hearts pickled in formaldehyde.
Preserved.
Oh, yes.
At Camp Buchenwald.
It was a laboratory.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS]
December 17, 1945.
In the States.
By December 15, 1945.
From Indiantown Gap, Pennsylvania.
Well, whenever we arrived in the States, they had a big dinner prepared for us at-- at Indiantown Gap, a steak dinner.
Potatoes and vegetables and so forth.
But there was something that my body was crying for.
It wasn't no meat and potatoes.
Milk.
Yeah.
And this mess sergeant told me that most of the soldiers coming back was craving this milk, fresh milk, because we had to use powdered milk the whole time we were over there.
And I took a number 56 dipper, which is about a quart, and I stood there.
They had a big 20-gallon pot with a spigot on it, merged in ice to keep it nice and cold.
And I take this number 56 dipper, which is about a quart, and I drank about four of those.
And they had-- was no room for no steak and potatoes.
[LAUGHTER] I just wanted milk.
And when I got home, my mother was getting two quarts of milk a day for the family.
I told her, put two more.
I'll pay for it.
Get two more quarts.
So she ordered two more quarts.

Then as I settled down, I would get in the mornings when I'd get up, I'd eat a half a pound of bacon, about six eggs, and drink a quart of milk for breakfast [INAUDIBLE] before I had eggs and bacon, mostly.
And then cereals, oatmeal, and all that sort of thing.
Let's see.
I got home in '45.
Three years later I got married.
I got married in '48.
October 6, 1948.
Yes, I have three children, three boys.
Well, my oldest son--
William Paul, Jr.
He has a degree in architecture.
He graduated University of Pennsylvania.
Got his degree in architecture.
Then he got his master's in ecology from Slippery Rock University.
My second son works for a board of education here in Pittsburgh, Pennsylvania.
He's a teacher.
He just got his certification-- oh-- I guess it was last year or-- he's a certified teacher now.
Before he was sub teaching.
Christopher.
He's deceased.
His name was Michael.
He had muscular dystrophy.
About four years now.
Seeing this Dr.
Danowski, who was in charge of research.
He was under his direct observation.
And Dr.
Danowski had told me the type of muscular dystrophy that he had.
A life expectancy of people with that type was between 35 and 40.
He lived to be 38.
He had his degree in business administration.
He was a tax accountant.
He worked for Internal Revenue downtown.
He worked for the city in their tax division.

And then, when he was working for Internal Revenue, they-- most of the other personnel working there, they would load his desk up with all this work, paperwork, whilst they goofed off at the water fountain and the coffee machine.
And I told him, no, you don't need that.
And so I made him give it up, because anytime you have a disability, you automatically get $150 Social Security.
And so I didn't think that was adequate for a young man, so I put him on my Social Security.
And so they took so much out of my Social Security and added to it and made him get about $300 of mine.
Yes.
I'd be delighted to meet them.
I'd be-- I'd be delighted to meet them.
Oh, you know, I would say how wonderful, fine young men you've grown up to be and so forth.
And just be overwhelmed to see that they had prospered.
And then--
Oh, I have no idea.
They would probably say thank you for feeding us [DRINKS] when we were hungry.
But I doubt very seriously.
I don't know where they are today.
Yes.
Why is it important?
Well, it's important to let the generation come-- coming along know that life is not all peaches and cream.
And some-- and to be a true red-blooded American, sometime you have to make certain sacrifices.
And making sacrifices is not fighting among each other and getting involved with the wrong people.
I never dreamed that I'd seen drugs and so forth destroying our communities and so forth.
I never dreamed that I'd see gangsters and racketeers taking over, getting the upper hand in our communities and so forth.
And even today, I-- I never thought that I would see the day come where every time you turn the television on, it's full with nothing but violence.
Yeah.
So what's the society coming to?
Is there anything else you would like to say that we may not have-- that you may not have spoken about before during your testimony?
No.
I was not liberated.
After I had completed my tour of duty in the military, well, after the war in Europe, I was in the Army Reserve.
And came out of the Army Reserve and went back and served an additional year, assisting the wounded soldiers at Howard General Hospital on Staten Island returning home.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And that's where I made sergeant.
No, I was discharged with the rank of PFC.
At Howard General Hospital, where I was promoted to sergeant, then I was-- then I came out, got discharged from-- I was getting ready to make a career out of the Army.
I got discharged because of family concerns.
I went to work.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I was up to be promoted to master sergeant and shipped back over to Germany because of my previous experience in Germany.
But I turned it down.
When I was discharged a second time, I turned it down, come home to assist with securing my family.
It's time to lend every effort of one's being in trying to establish peace and harmony throughout communities, throughout these United States of America, and throughout the world without bloodshed.
It's time that we come to our senses and eliminate bloodshed.
Because war is hell.
War is horrible.
[PAUSES FOR 7 SECONDS]
You're welcome.
